Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}}, in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ({{ill|1661|ja|1661st year }}), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家}} and the {{ill|Nijō Family|ja|二条家}} were relocated to the north side of {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通}}, outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over {{ill|impurity|ja|穢れ}}—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kyoto Magistrate|ja|京都町奉行}}—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 7 ({{ill|1694|ja|1694th year }}) onward, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}} imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 6 ({{ill|1721|ja|1721st year }}) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390
[[Category:Court Nobility]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Court Nobility in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:11]] [[ja:公家町]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the {{ill|court nobles|ja|公家|1=Kuge}} established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}・{{ill|Kyoto Imperial Palace|ja|京都御所}}) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, corresponding to the present-day {{ill|Kyoto Gyoen|ja|京都御苑}} and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In {{ill|Ancient|ja|古代}} capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the {{ill|Great Imperial Palace|ja|大内裏|1=Heian Palace}}. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏}} came to be used instead; during the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanbokuchō period|lt_ja=南北朝時代}}, one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位}}) of {{ill|Emperor Shōchō|ja|正親町天皇}} and the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} were being considered. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} 3 ({{ill|1575|ja|1575th year }}), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ({{ill|1585|ja|1585th year }}), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the {{ill|Jorakudai|ja|聚楽第}} as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the {{ill|Konoe Family|ja|近衛家}} was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ({{ill|1590|ja|1590th year }}), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, and during the siege of {{ill|Fushimi Castle|ja|伏見城}}—a precursor to the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}}—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 2 ({{ill|1597|ja|1597th year }}), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of {{ill|Kōdai-in|ja|高台院}}, and later, on its site, the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇}} was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 10 ({{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }}), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 16 ({{ill|1611|ja|1611th year }}) with the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} for {{ill|Emperor Goyōsei|ja|後陽成天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Goyōsei|lt_ja=後陽成上皇}}, the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通}}, {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the {{ill|Manji era|ja|万治}}, the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In {{ill|Shōō|ja|承応}} 2 ({{ill|1653|ja|1653rd year }}) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in {{ill|Hōei|ja|宝永}} 5 ({{ill|1708|ja|1708th year }}), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of {{ill|Sōkokuji|ja|相国寺}}).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通}}, {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通}}, and {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通}}—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家}}, {{ill|Reisen Family|ja|冷泉家}}, and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of {{ill|Ōtani Yoshitsugu|ja|大谷吉継}}, who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇}}, who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year }} on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}}), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family rank|ja|家格}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}}, in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ({{ill|1661|ja|1661st year }}), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家}} and the {{ill|Nijō Family|ja|二条家}} were relocated to the north side of {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通}}, outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over {{ill|impurity|ja|穢れ}}—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kyoto Magistrate|ja|京都町奉行}}—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 7 ({{ill|1694|ja|1694th year }}) onward, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}} imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 6 ({{ill|1721|ja|1721st year }}) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390

[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the {{ill|court nobles|ja|公家|1=Kuge}} established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}・{{ill|Kyoto Imperial Palace|ja|京都御所}}) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}}, corresponding to the present-day {{ill|Kyoto Gyoen|ja|京都御苑}} and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In {{ill|Ancient|ja|古代}} capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the {{ill|Great Imperial Palace|ja|大内裏|1=Heian Palace}}. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏}} came to be used instead; during the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanbokuchō period|lt_ja=南北朝時代}}, one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位}}) of {{ill|Emperor Shōchō|ja|正親町天皇}} and the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} were being considered. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era}} 3 ({{ill|1575|ja|1575th year }}), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}} presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ({{ill|1585|ja|1585th year }}), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the {{ill|Jorakudai|ja|聚楽第}} as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the {{ill|Konoe Family|ja|近衛家}} was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ({{ill|1590|ja|1590th year }}), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, and during the siege of {{ill|Fushimi Castle|ja|伏見城}}—a precursor to the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}}—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 2 ({{ill|1597|ja|1597th year }}), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of {{ill|Kōdai-in|ja|高台院}}, and later, on its site, the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇}} was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 10 ({{ill|1605|ja|1605th year }}), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 16 ({{ill|1611|ja|1611th year }}) with the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所}} for {{ill|Emperor Goyōsei|ja|後陽成天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Goyōsei|lt_ja=後陽成上皇}}, the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通}}, {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the {{ill|Manji era|ja|万治}}, the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In {{ill|Shōō|ja|承応}} 2 ({{ill|1653|ja|1653rd year }}) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in {{ill|Hōei|ja|宝永}} 5 ({{ill|1708|ja|1708th year }}), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of {{ill|Sōkokuji|ja|相国寺}}).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通}}, {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通}}, and {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通}}—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}, acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家}}, {{ill|Reisen Family|ja|冷泉家}}, and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of {{ill|Ōtani Yoshitsugu|ja|大谷吉継}}, who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永}}, as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇}}, who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year }} on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}}), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family rank|ja|家格}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}}, in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ({{ill|1661|ja|1661st year }}), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家}} and the {{ill|Nijō Family|ja|二条家}} were relocated to the north side of {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通}}, outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over {{ill|impurity|ja|穢れ}}—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kyoto Magistrate|ja|京都町奉行}}—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 7 ({{ill|1694|ja|1694th year }}) onward, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代}} imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 6 ({{ill|1721|ja|1721st year }}) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390

revision.text:
[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the {{ill|court nobles|ja|公家|1=Kuge|12=draft}} established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}・{{ill|Kyoto Imperial Palace|ja|京都御所|12=draft}}) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, corresponding to the present-day {{ill|Kyoto Gyoen|ja|京都御苑|12=draft}} and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In {{ill|Ancient|ja|古代|12=draft}} capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城|12=draft}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司|12=draft}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the {{ill|Great Imperial Palace|ja|大内裏|1=Heian Palace|12=draft}}. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京|12=draft}} fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏|12=draft}} came to be used instead; during the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanbokuchō period|lt_ja=南北朝時代|12=draft}}, one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路|12=draft}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路|12=draft}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨|12=draft}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権|12=draft}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Emperor Shōchō|ja|正親町天皇|12=draft}} and the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} were being considered. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1575|ja|1575th year|12=draft}}), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft}} presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記|12=draft}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権|12=draft}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ({{ill|1585|ja|1585th year|12=draft}}), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=draft}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the {{ill|Jorakudai|ja|聚楽第|12=draft}} as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割|12=draft}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the {{ill|Konoe Family|ja|近衛家|12=draft}} was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売|12=draft}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ({{ill|1590|ja|1590th year|12=draft}}), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家|12=draft}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え|12=draft}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構|12=draft}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|12=draft}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} era, and during the siege of {{ill|Fushimi Castle|ja|伏見城|12=draft}}—a precursor to the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}}—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1597|ja|1597th year|12=draft}}), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城|12=draft}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of {{ill|Kōdai-in|ja|高台院|12=draft}}, and later, on its site, the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇|12=draft}} was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 10 ({{ill|1605|ja|1605th year|12=draft}}), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 16 ({{ill|1611|ja|1611th year|12=draft}}) with the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} for {{ill|Emperor Goyōsei|ja|後陽成天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Goyōsei|lt_ja=後陽成上皇|12=draft}}, the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地|12=draft}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).

During the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永|12=draft}}, the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋|12=draft}}—were fixed.

By the latest during the {{ill|Manji era|ja|万治|12=draft}}, the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In {{ill|Shōō|ja|承応|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1653|ja|1653rd year|12=draft}}) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in {{ill|Hōei|ja|宝永|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1708|ja|1708th year|12=draft}}), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|12=draft}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of {{ill|Sōkokuji|ja|相国寺|12=draft}}).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通|12=draft}}—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}}, acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家|12=draft}}, {{ill|Reisen Family|ja|冷泉家|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家|12=draft}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘|12=draft}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of {{ill|Ōtani Yoshitsugu|ja|大谷吉継|12=draft}}, who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤|12=draft}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永|12=draft}}, even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|12=draft}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the {{ill|Bunroku|ja|文禄|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永|12=draft}}, as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇|12=draft}}, who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in {{ill|1630|ja|1630th year|12=draft}} on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代|12=draft}}), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|12=draft}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|12=draft}} era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family rank|ja|家格|12=draft}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代|12=draft}}, in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|12=draft}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ({{ill|1661|ja|1661st year|12=draft}}), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Nijō Family|ja|二条家|12=draft}} were relocated to the north side of {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通|12=draft}}, outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over {{ill|impurity|ja|穢れ|12=draft}}—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Kyoto Magistrate|ja|京都町奉行|12=draft}}—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役|12=draft}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}} 7 ({{ill|1694|ja|1694th year|12=draft}}) onward, the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|ja|京都所司代|12=draft}} imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|12=draft}} 6 ({{ill|1721|ja|1721st year|12=draft}}) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|12=draft}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390
{{draft categories| [[Category:Court Nobility]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Court Nobility in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kuge towns]] [[ja:公家町]]

[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the {{ill|court nobles|ja|公家|1=Kuge|12=draft}} established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}・{{ill|Kyoto Imperial Palace|ja|京都御所|12=draft}}) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}}, corresponding to the present-day {{ill|Kyoto Gyoen|ja|京都御苑|12=draft}} and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In {{ill|Ancient|ja|古代|12=draft}} capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城|12=draft}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司|12=draft}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the {{ill|Great Imperial Palace|ja|大内裏|1=Heian Palace|12=draft}}. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京|12=draft}} fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏|12=draft}} came to be used instead; during the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period (Japan)|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanbokuchō period|lt_ja=南北朝時代|12=draft}}, one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|12=draft}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路|12=draft}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路|12=draft}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨|12=draft}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権|12=draft}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Emperor Shōchō|ja|正親町天皇|12=draft}} and the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} were being considered. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1575|ja|1575th year|12=draft}}), {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft}} presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記|12=draft}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権|12=draft}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ({{ill|1585|ja|1585th year|12=draft}}), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|12=draft}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the {{ill|Jorakudai|ja|聚楽第|12=draft}} as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割|12=draft}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the {{ill|Konoe Family|ja|近衛家|12=draft}} was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売|12=draft}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ({{ill|1590|ja|1590th year|12=draft}}), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家|12=draft}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え|12=draft}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構|12=draft}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|12=draft}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} era, and during the siege of {{ill|Fushimi Castle|ja|伏見城|12=draft}}—a precursor to the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|ja|関ヶ原の戦い|12=draft}}—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1597|ja|1597th year|12=draft}}), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城|12=draft}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of {{ill|Kōdai-in|ja|高台院|12=draft}}, and later, on its site, the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} of {{ill|Emperor Gosui|ja|後水尾天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Gosui|lt_ja=後水尾上皇|12=draft}} was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 10 ({{ill|1605|ja|1605th year|12=draft}}), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 16 ({{ill|1611|ja|1611th year|12=draft}}) with the construction of the {{ill|Sendō Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}} for {{ill|Emperor Goyōsei|ja|後陽成天皇|lt=Retired Emperor Goyōsei|lt_ja=後陽成上皇|12=draft}}, the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地|12=draft}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the {{ill|Kan'ei period|ja|寛永|12=draft}}, the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋|12=draft}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the {{ill|Manji era|ja|万治|12=draft}}, the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In {{ill|Shōō|ja|承応|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1653|ja|1653rd year|12=draft}}) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in {{ill|Hōei|ja|宝永|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1708|ja|1708th year|12=draft}}), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|12=draft}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of {{ill|Sōkokuji|ja|相国寺|12=draft}}).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by {{ill|Teramachi-dōri|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Karasuma-dōri|ja|烏丸通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Imadegawa-dōri|ja|今出川通|12=draft}}—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
