[[sn:Kuge towns]] [[ja:公家町]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の公家]]

[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}・[[:en:Kyoto Imperial Palace|Kyoto Imperial Palace]]) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, corresponding to the present-day [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen]] and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient]] capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|qq=}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government offices|qq=}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinairi|qq=}} came to be used instead; during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanbokuchō period]], one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō Ōji|qq=}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banri Kōji|qq=}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsukasa Kōji|qq=}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasuma Kōji|qq=}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyūsha Senji|qq=}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Regime|qq=}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abdication|qq=}}) of [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Shōchō]] and the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] were being considered. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 3 ([[:en:1575|1575]]), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyudendō Nikki|qq=}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Regime|qq=}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ([[:en:1585|1585]]), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|qq=}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the [[:en:Jurakudai|Jorakudai]] as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Land Survey|qq=}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe Family]] was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetachuri|qq=}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imadegawa|qq=}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ([[:en:1590|1590]]), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachijō Family|qq=}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Horse Muster|qq=}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkō|qq=}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era, and during the siege of [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]]—a precursor to the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]]—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 2 ([[:en:1597|1597]]), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Shinjo|qq=}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of [[:en:Kōdai-in|Kōdai-in]], and later, on its site, the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]] was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 10 ([[:en:1605|1605]]), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 16 ([[:en:1611|1611]]) with the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] for [[:en:Emperor Go-Yōzei|Retired Emperor Goyōsei]], the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/original site|qq=}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|qq=}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the [[:en:Manji (era)|Manji era]], the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In [[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Shōō]] 2 ([[:en:1653|1653]]) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in [[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 5 ([[:en:1708|1708]]), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of [[:en:Shōkoku-ji|Sōkokuji]]).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}, and [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]]—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Family|qq=}}, [[:en:Reizei family|Reisen Family]], and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō Family|qq=}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial decree|qq=}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of [[:en:Ōtani Yoshitsugu|Ōtani Yoshitsugu]], who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Katsutane|qq=}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]], who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in [[:en:1630|1630]] on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]]), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family Rank|ja|家格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family Rank|qq=}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]], in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ([[:en:1661|1661]]), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi-no-miya Family|qq=}} and the [[:en:Nijō family|Nijō Family]] were relocated to the north side of [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]], outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over [[:en:Kegare|impurity]]—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Magistrate]]—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/town duties|qq=}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 7 ([[:en:1694|1694]]) onward, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 6 ([[:en:1721|1721]]) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390

{{draft categories| [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Court Nobility in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]]<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->[[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:qq]]

[[sn:Kuge towns]] [[ja:公家町]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の公家]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}・[[:en:Kyoto Imperial Palace|Kyoto Imperial Palace]]) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, corresponding to the present-day [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen]] and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient]] capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|qq=}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government offices|qq=}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinairi|qq=}} came to be used instead; during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanbokuchō period]], one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一条通}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banri Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳馬場通}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsukasa Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasuma Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 烏丸通}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyūsha Senji|qq=}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Regime|qq=}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abdication|qq=}}) of [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Shōchō]] and the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] were being considered. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 3 ([[:en:1575|1575]]), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyudendō Nikki|qq=}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Regime|qq=}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ([[:en:1585|1585]]), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|qq=}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the [[:en:Jurakudai|Jorakudai]] as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Land Survey|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正の地割}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe Family]] was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetachuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imadegawa|qq=}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ([[:en:1590|1590]]), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachijō Family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 桂宮}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Horse Muster|qq=}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era, and during the siege of [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]]—a precursor to the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]]—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 2 ([[:en:1597|1597]]), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Shinjo|qq=}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of [[:en:Kōdai-in|Kōdai-in]], and later, on its site, the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]] was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 10 ([[:en:1605|1605]]), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 16 ([[:en:1611|1611]]) with the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] for [[:en:Emperor Go-Yōzei|Retired Emperor Goyōsei]], the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/original site|qq=}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the [[:en:Manji (era)|Manji era]], the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In [[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Shōō]] 2 ([[:en:1653|1653]]) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in [[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 5 ([[:en:1708|1708]]), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of [[:en:Shōkoku-ji|Sōkokuji]]).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}, and [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]]—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Family|qq=}}, [[:en:Reizei family|Reisen Family]], and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō Family|qq=}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial decree|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勘事}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of [[:en:Ōtani Yoshitsugu|Ōtani Yoshitsugu]], who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Katsutane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 原長頼}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]], who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in [[:en:1630|1630]] on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]]), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family Rank|ja|家格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family Rank|qq=}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]], in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ([[:en:1661|1661]]), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi-no-miya Family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伏見宮}} and the [[:en:Nijō family|Nijō Family]] were relocated to the north side of [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]], outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over [[:en:Kegare|impurity]]—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Magistrate]]—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/town duties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 7 ([[:en:1694|1694]]) onward, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 6 ([[:en:1721|1721]]) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390
{{translated page|ja|公家町|version=99614974|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:History of Kamigyō Ward]] [[Category:Court Nobility in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo Period]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]]<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->[[sn:Kuge towns]][[Category:qq]]
}}
[[sn:Kuge towns]] [[ja:公家町]] [[Category:History of Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の公家]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Court Nobility in the Edo Period
revision.text:
[[File:Kuge-machi map.jpg|thumb|350px|Kuge town in the [[Bakumatsu]] period]] '''Kuge Machi''' (公家町) is the residential area for the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] established in the vicinity of the Imperial Palace ({{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}・[[:en:Kyoto Imperial Palace|Kyoto Imperial Palace]]) in Early Modern {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}, corresponding to the present-day [[:en:Kyoto Gyoen National Garden|Kyoto Gyoen]] and its surrounding areas.
== The Establishment of Kuge Machi == In [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient]] capitals ({{ill|capital|ja|都城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/capital|qq=}}), government offices ({{ill|government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government offices|qq=}}) and related facilities were arranged around the Imperial Palace—the Emperor’s residence—to enclose it and form the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]. However, in the medieval period the Imperial Palace in [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] fell into ruin, and the {{ill|Rinairi|ja|里内裏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinairi|qq=}} came to be used instead; during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanbokuchō period]], one such palace, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, was fixed as the Imperial Palace.
Although no additional government offices or outer enclosures (such as fortifications) existed around {{ill|Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|ja|土御門東洞院殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado Higashitōin-dono|qq=}}, the area of one chō square (amounting to an area equivalent to three chō square) around the Imperial Palace—surrounded by {{ill|Ichijō Ōji|ja|一条大路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一条通}}, {{ill|Banri Kōji|ja|万里小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banri Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 柳馬場通}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa Kōji|ja|鷹司小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takatsukasa Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Karasuma Kōji|ja|烏丸小路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasuma Kōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 烏丸通}}—was designated as the zone of the Great Imperial Palace and called “Jinchū”. Regulations were imposed analogous to those within the palace proper, such as prohibiting the passage (for both officials and civilians) by any vehicle lacking the {{ill|Gyūsha Senji|ja|牛車宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyūsha Senji|qq=}}.<ref>桃崎有一郎「中世里内裏の空間構造と〈陣〉」・「中世里内裏陣中の構造と空間的性質」『中世京都の空間構造と礼節体系』（思文閣出版、2010th year ） ISBN 978-4-7842-1502-7</ref>
It is generally believed that the plan to concentrate the court nobles around the Imperial Palace—that is, the establishment of Kuge Machi—was carried out in conjunction with [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]’s remodeling of Kyoto. However, in reality the concept had existed since the time under the {{ill|Oda Regime|ja|織田政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Regime|qq=}}, when the abdication ({{ill|abdication|ja|譲位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abdication|qq=}}) of [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Shōchō]] and the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] were being considered. In [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō Era]] 3 ([[:en:1575|1575]]), [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]] presented Emperor Shōchō with a plan to gather the court nobles—including the regent families—on the east and south sides of the Imperial Palace, to which the Emperor consented (as recorded in the {{ill|Goyudendō Nikki|ja|御湯殿上日記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyudendō Nikki|qq=}} entry of July 13, Tenshō 3).<ref name="#1">山口、2017th year 、P385</ref>
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi Regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Regime|qq=}}, the actual gathering of the court nobles began in parallel with the construction of the Sendō Palace for the retired Emperor Shōchō in Tenshō 13 ([[:en:1585|1585]]), and after Hideyoshi’s appointment as {{ill|Kampaku|ja|関白|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kampaku|qq=}}, it was positioned prior to the subsequent phases of Kyoto’s remodeling—such as the construction of the [[:en:Jurakudai|Jorakudai]] as the government office, the formation of the temple town, the construction of the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}}, and the {{ill|Tenshō Land Survey|ja|天正地割|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō Land Survey|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正の地割}}—and is regarded rather as one of the court maneuvers carried out during the establishment of the Toyotomi regime.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P31 - 41</ref> As a precursor, in the autumn of Tenshō 13 the residence of the [[:en:Konoe family|Konoe Family]] was relocated from {{ill|Uetachuri|ja|上立売|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetachuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Imadegawa|ja|今出川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imadegawa|qq=}}.<ref name="#1"/> Later, with Hideyoshi’s appointment as Kampaku, the policy of gathering the court nobles was also expected to visibly manifest their service to the court.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51">登谷、2015th year 、P51</ref>
Subsequently, in Tenshō 18 ([[:en:1590|1590]]), when the establishment of the {{ill|Hachijō Family|ja|八条宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachijō Family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 桂宮}} was decided, the court nobles residing in that area were relocated to make way for the construction of a palace on the north side of the Imperial Palace, and a new residential area for the court nobles was established on its east side.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref> Moreover, this district had already been considered a candidate for the settlement of the court nobles—as, during the Oda Regime, central Kyoto had fallen into ruin and become fields and vacant lots—and was once the site where Oda Nobunaga conducted the {{ill|Kyoto Horse Muster|ja|京都御馬揃え|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Horse Muster|qq=}}.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P34 - 36・41 - 44・65</ref>
Furthermore, a structure known as {{ill|Sōkō|ja|惣構|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}}, built to nearly enclose and protect the area formerly called “Jinchū” around the Imperial Palace, was constructed. Since its construction coincided with the period when the {{ill|Imperial Outworks|ja|御土居|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Outworks|qq=}} were built in central Kyoto, it is highly likely that it was established at that time (at the very least, the Sōkō already existed during the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] era, and during the siege of [[:en:Fushimi Castle|Fushimi Castle]]—a precursor to the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]]—its main gate was closed).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P72 - 80</ref>
Also, in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 2 ([[:en:1597|1597]]), Hideyoshi constructed a new castle for Kyoto ({{ill|Kyoto Shinjo|ja|京都新城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Shinjo|qq=}}) on the southeast side of the Imperial Palace as a facility to replace the Jorakudai; this structure became the residence of [[:en:Kōdai-in|Kōdai-in]], and later, on its site, the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]] was built.<ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386">山口、2017th year 、P386</ref>
{{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} essentially inherited the policies of the Toyotomi Regime. In [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 10 ([[:en:1605|1605]]), with the reconstruction of the Imperial Palace, and in [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 16 ([[:en:1611|1611]]) with the construction of the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sendō Palace]] for [[:en:Emperor Go-Yōzei|Retired Emperor Goyōsei]], the residences, temples, and townhouses (the “{{ill|original site|ja|上地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/original site|qq=}}”) on the target land were relocated, and the residential area of the court nobles expanded further east (up to that point, even as the settlement of the court nobles progressed, the residences of samurai and townspeople coexisted).
Furthermore, by expanding the existing Sōkō, a new Sōkō was established, thereby enlarging the area of Kuge Machi within the Sōkō.
During the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], the expansion of the Sōkō was almost completed, and the boundaries of the area—surrounded by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|ja|北土御門通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita-Tsuchimikado-dōri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|ja|相国寺石橋南町筋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkokuji Ishibashi-Nancho-suji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—were fixed.
As the settlement area of the court nobles expanded beyond the former “Jinchū” and was further enclosed by the Sōkō, markers known as “Jinseki” were placed; however, since Jinchū had become almost an abstract concept, the Sōkō and its accompanying main gate came to be recognized as the outer boundary enclosing the Imperial Palace, and were called “Sōmon no Uchi.”
By the latest during the [[:en:Manji (era)|Manji era]], the Sōkō—with its lingering traces of the Sengoku period—came to be called “Chikujinochi”, and the term “Chikujinochi” became established as an alternative name for Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P42 - 51・81 - 89・96</ref>
In [[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Shōō]] 2 ([[:en:1653|1653]]) a fire destroyed the Imperial Palace, and thereafter Kuge Machi was repeatedly struck by fires. Then, in [[:en:Hōei|Hōei]] 5 ([[:en:1708|1708]]), a fire burned down almost the entire area of Kuge Machi—including the Imperial Palace and Sendō Palace. Capitalizing on this disaster, roads were constructed through Kuge Machi and some court noble residences were relocated to the southern adjacent area of the Sōkō (Chikujinochi), thereby effectively incorporating them into “Chikujinochi.” (Including the vacant lots provided as firebreaks, Kuge Machi itself extended southward to {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}.) A major reconstruction was thus carried out (it is also noted that the southward expansion was due to the fact that further expansion to the north was impossible as it abutted the grounds of [[:en:Shōkoku-ji|Sōkokuji]]).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P97・172 - 178・186 - 192</ref>
It can be said that at this point the extent of Kuge Machi—which connects to Kyoto Gyoen from the Meiji period onward and is enclosed by [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi-dōri]], [[:en:Karasuma Street|Karasuma-dōri]], {{ill|Marutamachi-dōri|ja|丸太町通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marutamachi-dōri|qq=}}, and [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]]—was established.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P193</ref> (Note: parts on the east side of Teramachi-dōri continued to house court noble residences that could not be otherwise accommodated.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P205 - 208</ref>)
== The Reality of Kuge Machi == However, it is not true that the Oda, Toyotomi, and Tokugawa ({{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) regimes confined the court nobles within the insular space of Kuge Machi by forcibly gathering them together. Rather, in reality, many court nobles were unable to reside in Kuge Machi.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P51"/>
Immediately after the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], acting on his own arrangements, granted the residences of the {{ill|Yamashina Family|ja|山科家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Family|qq=}}, [[:en:Reizei family|Reisen Family]], and {{ill|Shijō Family|ja|四条家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijō Family|qq=}}—which had been allowed to return to Kyoto after having had the imperial decree rescinded ({{ill|imperial decree|ja|勅勘|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial decree|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勘事}})—to the north side of Kuge Machi. This involved the confiscation of the former residences of figures such as the mother (Tōden) of [[:en:Ōtani Yoshitsugu|Ōtani Yoshitsugu]], who perished with the Western Army, and {{ill|Hara Katsutane|ja|原勝胤|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hara Katsutane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 原長頼}}, and is said to have been motivated by the inability to secure new residence sites within Kuge Machi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P45</ref>
Subsequently, although Kuge Machi was expanded from the [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], even more new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) were established. Under the Toyotomi Regime from the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] to the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei period]], as many as fifty new families were founded; however, 33 of these (two‐thirds) were unable to secure residences within Chikujinochi—that is, within Kuge Machi (from contemporary maps, nearly 70% of the residential areas in Kuge Machi excluding the palace belonged to the imperial family, regent families, Seika families, and old noble families). Moreover, among the new families that did secure residences within Kuge Machi, only those closely associated with the retired court (such as the attendants of [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Retired Emperor Gosui]], who was conducting the retired court system at that time) obtained such lands; when the Sendō Palace of Retired Emperor Gosui was completed in [[:en:1630|1630]] on the south side of the palace, residences for the retired court and the imperial princesses were also constructed, along with the granting of residence lands to the retired attendants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P109 - 114</ref><ref name="山口、2017th year 、P386"/>
Consequently, court nobles unable to reside in Kuge Machi had no choice but either to receive a grant of a residence from the Emperor or the retired court (in practice, via the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]]), to cohabit in the residence of a relative, to purchase land in nearby towns and build a residence, or to rent a house. Furthermore, even those who owned residence lands in Kuge Machi sometimes sold them due to economic difficulties and ended up renting outside Chikujinochi.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P115 - 120</ref> Particularly after the onset of the Early Modern period, while the establishment of new families ({{ill|new families|ja|新家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/new families|qq=}}) was necessitated by the demands of court administration, the allocation of stipend lands and residences lagged behind.<ref>山口、2017th year 、P388</ref>
Moreover, a significant disparity emerged in how families coped with the loss of their residences by fire—between those with daimyō among their relatives who could obtain economic assistance and those who had to rely solely on income from stipend lands. By the [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] era, while court nobles connected by daimyō kinship constructed lavish residences that did not conform to their {{ill|family Rank|ja|家格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family Rank|qq=}}, prompting the shogunate to impose regulations, those owning properties within Chikujinochi, having abandoned rebuilding their burned residences, began to reside in town areas. In response, the shogunate planned to reorganize the sites of these defunct residences; however, because neither the court nor the shogunate had a firm grasp on the transfer of property rights between court nobles, the target sites could not be finalized, leading to failures.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P217 - 227</ref>
As for court noble residences, the shogunate maintained the view that, with the exception of families compelled by their hereditary duties to reside in stipend lands, it was preferable for court nobles to reside within Kuge Machi or its vicinity in connection with their service to the court. Accordingly, when a major fire struck Kyoto and land reorganization issues arose, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]], in consultation with the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}, took measures to incorporate by reassigning temple lands and lands inhabited by townspeople around Kuge Machi—thus enabling court nobles without established residences in or around Kuge Machi to obtain new grants.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref> (In fact, following the fire in Manji 4 ([[:en:1661|1661]]), the residences of the {{ill|Fushimi-no-miya Family|ja|伏見宮家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi-no-miya Family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伏見宮}} and the [[:en:Nijō family|Nijō Family]] were relocated to the north side of [[:en:Imadegawa Street|Imadegawa-dōri]], outside Chikujinochi.)<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P121 - 128・151</ref>
On the other hand, since many court nobles objected—due to concerns over [[:en:Kegare|impurity]]—to residing on lands formerly occupied by temples, graveyards, or neighboring noble residences, the shogunate recognized this position and undertook measures such as altering residence sites and carrying out earth replacement works.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P125 - 126・150</ref> Moreover, because the dense concentration of court noble residences in Kuge Machi resulted in insufficient firebreaks and narrow passageways, making them vulnerable to fires, measures had to be taken to expand these areas.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P146 - 148</ref> Numerous problems of this nature meant that the policy of gathering all court nobles within and around Kuge Machi could not be fully realized. Consequently, although court nobles who could not reside in or around Kuge Machi were permitted to live elsewhere within central Kyoto, their residences—being outside the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Magistrate]]—could not be accessed by magistrate officials during incidents, thereby hindering criminal investigations.<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P155 - 157</ref> Furthermore, concerns over the deterioration of court decorum<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P158</ref> and the fact that the town duties ({{ill|town duties|ja|町役|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/town duties|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) burdened by the townspeople were not assumed by the court nobles led to conflicts between residents and the nobles (although there were cases where a court noble’s retainer acted on behalf of town duties or where the town permitted residence in exchange for assuming the financial burden of town duties).<ref>登谷、2015th year 、P212 - 214・266 - 273・288 - 291</ref>
For this reason, from [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 7 ([[:en:1694|1694]]) onward, the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] imposed an obligation to register for those court nobles residing in town areas,<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref> and then in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] 6 ([[:en:1721|1721]]) regulations were imposed whereby, regardless of whether the land was granted, leased, or purchased, if a court noble wished to exchange properties (sōtaika), he had to obtain the permission of the Shoshidai through the {{ill|Samurai Envoy|ja|武家伝奏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Envoy|qq=}}. However, since it was impossible to achieve the policy of concentrating court nobles in Kuge Machi due to the inherent difficulty in securing land, no further regulations banning court nobles from residing in town areas could be imposed.<ref name="登谷、2015th year 、P154">登谷、2015th year 、P154 - 159</ref>
== Footnotes == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Nobuhiro Todoya, ''Court Society in Early Modern Japan and Kyoto: Forms of Concentration and Urban Society'' (Shibunkaku Publishing, 2015) ISBN 978-4-7842-1795-3 * Kazuo Yamaguchi, “The Court and Court Nobility Society” (first published in History Society & Japanese History Society ed., ''Japanese History Lecture Series 6: Early Modern Society'' (Tokyo University Press, 2005) / included in Yamaguchi, ''Early Modern Japanese Political History and the Court'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2017) ISBN 978-4-642-03480-7), Chapter 5 “Kuge Machi of Early Modern Kyoto,” 2017, pp. 385–390
{{translated page|ja|公家町|version=99614974|comment=Imported full ja history}}
